Sport|Olympics

Team GBended theParis Olympicslying a relatively lowly seventh in the overall medals table, with 65, a noticeable drop in the standings, having been third, second and fourth in London, Rio and Tokyo respectively.

Thathaul eclipsed Tokyo, matched London and was just two down on Rio de Janeiro, but with far less of a golden hue than all three, with just 14 in all, comfortably less that those preceding trio of Olympics.

British Olympic Association CEO Andy Anson said: “Sixty five medals is our second-best ever tally on foreign soil so that’s something incredible to celebrate."

With Paris having drawn to a close, we assess Team GB sport by sport.

Britain’s swimmers enjoyed their best-ever Games in Tokyo, and following that was always going to be a huge ask.

Five medals was a decent return in Paris, but there will be frustration that only one was gold (the men’s 4x200m freestyle), after both Adam Peaty and Matt Richards were denied by two-hundredths of a second. Britain’s divers won five medals - more than at any Games - including a sweep of podium places in the synchro events.

Olympics closing ceremony: Tom Cruise, Snoop Dogg and Billie Eilish all feature in star-studded Paris finale

Olympics 2024: How Georgia Bell produced remarkable comeback to win fairytale Paris bronze

Olympics 2024 final medal table as Team GB surpass Tokyo haul with fewer golds

Given China’s dominance, that is quite some going. On the final weekend, an unexpected third source emerged as Kate Shortman and Izzy Thorpe won the country’s first artistic swimming medal.

Ten medals represented Britain’s best haul since 1984. Keely Hodgkinson’s800m triumph was the highlight, as she became Britain’s first Olympic athletics champion since Mo Farah in Rio.

That neither Josh Kerr nor Matthew Hudson-Smith could follow suit is a disappointment on paper, but both ran British records and missed out by fractions in epic races. It’s hard to criticise.

Medals for Katarina Johnson-Thompson and Georgia Bell were both brilliantly inspirational stories, and then there were the relays, where five medals out of five showcased Britain’s sprint depth and offered reward for taking every race seriously.

Molly Caudrey’s failure to reach the pole vault final was the low.

Paris was a rarity for the British cycling team, in that they did not top the medal table, despite picking up the biggest haul of any team, 11 in total.

It was a medal count perhaps higher than some had anticipated, and yet distinctly lacking gold, the only ones to come from Emma Finucane and her fellow sprinters in the team sprint and Tom Pidcock in hismost remarkable mountain bike comeback.

Beth Shriever seemed the big missed opportunity in the BMX racing, finishing dead last, having dominated the entire competition up until that point. On the track, the absence of Katie Archibald was notable.

A cloud of controversy hung over the entire equestrian team before the Games had even begun, when a film emerged of Charlotte Dujardin repeatedly whipping a horse.

She pulled out of the Games just before being suspended and it left the team minus a strong gold medal contender in both the individual and team dressage. T

hey still came away with two dressage bronze, with Charlotte Fry starring on both occasions. And there were further team equestrian medals – both gold – in the showjumping and three-day eventing. In the former, Harry Charles emulated dad Peter, who won gold at London 2012 with Scott Brash and Ben Maher, Charles’ team-mates here in Paris.

This was always billed as something of a transitional Games for Britain’s artistic gymnasts, with Max Whitlock bowing out and the women’s team missing several star names through injury. Both teams finished fourth in the team finals, despite the men’s being picked with individual medals in mind.

Whitlock missed out on one of those in the pommel horse, but Harry Hepworth and Jake Jarman showed they have hugely promising futures bymaking the podium at their first Games.

It was left to trampoline star Bryony Page, though, to deliver gold, the 33-year-old producing a sensational performance to complete her set of Olympic medals.

Rowing at the Tokyo Olympics had been an unmitigated disaster. The take-out message was judge us on Paris, where they ended up with eight medals in total, including gold in the women’s lightweight double sculls, women’s quadruple sculls and men’s eight.

There was disappointment when the men’s pair and the women’s four, including Helen Glover going for a third gold, were just pipped to the gold. In the kayak cross, Kimberley Woods took double bronze, while Joe Clarke and Adam Burgess won a silver apiece.

This was Britain’s first Olympics without at least one of Jonny or Alastair Brownlee since the brothers put the sport on the British Olympic map at London 2012. The success, though, continued, as Team GB finished top of the sport’s medal table for the fourth Games in a row.

Alex Yee was the star, upgrading his Tokyo silver togold with a devastating finish in the men’s race,while Beth Potter won bronze in the women’s. Only finishing third in the mixed relay, though, was probably below expectation, given favourites France crashed early on.

Team GB picked up medals in a raft of sports, with 19-year-old Toby Roberts clinching a surprise gold in sport climbing and fellow teenager Sky Brownwinning bronze in the skateboarding.

In the shooting, Nathan Hales won the men’s trap, while Amber Rutter, just a few months after giving birth to son Tommy,sealed skeet silver after a VAR controversy. Another Tommy, Fleetwood, could not fend off Scottie Scheffler in the golf, but still won silver, and Lewis Richardson was the sole British boxer to win a medal, bronze, amid much controversy.

And there was gold and bronze in sailing, courtesy of Ellie Aldridge and Emma Wilson respectively.

title: Olympics 2024: How Team GB performed across every sport at Paris Games | The Standard
author: Matt Majendie, Malik Ouzia
description: British athletes finish seventh in medal table - but match haul of London 2012
